summary: American poet known for character studies set in the fictional Tilbury Town, modeled after Gardiner, Maine. His most famous poems include 'Richard Cory,' 'Miniver Cheevy,' 'Mr. Flood's Party,' and 'Luke Havergal.' He won three Pulitzer Prizes in the 1920s.

works.name: Richard Cory
works.description: Robinson's most famous poem about a 'gentleman from sole to crown' who is 'rich -- yes, richer than a king.' Despite his outward perfection, he 'one calm summer night, went home and put a bullet through his head.' The poem is a study of the gap between outward prosperity and inner despair.

clues.clue: A wealthy man, 'a gentleman from sole to crown,' puts 'a bullet through his head'
examples: a man 'rich--yes, richer than a king' going home to 'put a bullet through his head'

works.name: Miniver Cheevy
works.description: A poem about a nostalgic malcontent who 'loved the Medici / Albeit he had never seen one,' mourned 'Romance, now on the town, and Art, a vagrant,' dreamed of Thebes and Camelot, and 'wept that he was ever born.' He is a 'child of scorn' who 'kept on drinking' as consolation for being born in the wrong era.

clues.clue: 'Loved the Medici / Albeit he had never seen one'; dreamed of Thebes and Camelot
examples: 'loved the Medici/Albeit he had never seen one'

clues.clue: A 'child of scorn' who lamented being born in the wrong generation and 'kept on drinking'
examples: a 'child of scorn' that was 'born too late' and thus 'kept on drinking'

clues.clue: Mourned 'Romance, now on the town, and Art, a vagrant'
examples: mourned Romance, now on the town, and Art, a vagrant

works.name: Mr. Flood's Party
works.description: A poem about an elderly man who climbs a hill and has a drink with himself, compared to 'Roland's ghost winding a silent horn.' He 'stood there in the middle of the road' looking down at a town 'where strangers would have shut the many doors that many friends had opened long ago.' He 'lifted up his voice and sang... for auld lang syne.'

clues.clue: An old man stops in the road and drinks alone, compared to 'Roland's ghost winding a silent horn'
examples: compared to Roland as he 'stood there in the middle of the road'
a figure who stops in the road and has a drink with himself

clues.clue: He sings 'for auld lang syne' with 'only two moons listening'
examples: 'lifted up his voice and sang, secure, with only two moons listening'

works.name: Luke Havergal
works.description: A dark poem from The Children of the Night that commands its title figure to 'Go to the western gate' where 'the vines cling crimson on the wall.' Contains the line 'hell is more than half of paradise' (or 'half of paradise'). The speaker comes 'out of the grave' to urge Luke onward, suggesting themes of suicide or the afterlife.

clues.clue: Commands 'Go to the western gate' where 'vines cling crimson on the wall'; 'hell is half of paradise'
examples: 'Go to the western gate... where the vines cling crimson on the wall'
'hell is more than half of paradise'

clues.clue: From the collection The Children of the Night
examples: a poem from his collection 'Children of the Night'

works.name: How Annandale Went Out
works.description: A sonnet about euthanasia in which a physician recalls using 'a slight kind of engine' and asks 'You wouldn't hang me? I thought not.' Annandale, husband of Miriam and Damaris, is 'the only man who never changed.'

clues.clue: A physician uses 'a slight kind of engine' and asks 'You wouldn't hang me?'
examples: a physician recalls how he used 'a slight kind of engine'
euthanized by his friend in a sonnet

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Robinson set many poems in the fictional Tilbury Town, modeled after Gardiner, Maine. He won three Pulitzer Prizes. His collections include The Children of the Night and The Man Against the Sky. 'The House on the Hill' is a villanelle whose tercets end with 'They are all gone away' or 'There is nothing more to say.' 'Eros Turannos' describes a woman who won't leave her husband because 'all her doubts of what he says are dimmed with what she knows of days.' Louise Bogan wrote a critical reevaluation titled Tilbury Town and Beyond.

clues.clue: Set poems in Tilbury Town, modeled after Gardiner, Maine
examples: Tilbury Town, modeled after Gardiner, Maine

clues.clue: Won three Pulitzer Prizes in the 1920s
'The House on the Hill' is a villanelle: 'They are all gone away' / 'There is nothing more to say'
examples: each of its tercets ends with 'They are all gone away' or 'There is nothing more to say'

comprehensive_summary:
Edwin Arlington Robinson is an American poet known for psychologically penetrating character studies set in the fictional Tilbury Town, modeled after Gardiner, Maine. His most famous poem, 'Richard Cory,' describes a wealthy 'gentleman from sole to crown' who shockingly 'one calm summer night, went home and put a bullet through his head.'

'Miniver Cheevy' portrays a nostalgic malcontent who 'loved the Medici / Albeit he had never seen one,' dreamed of Thebes and Camelot, mourned 'Romance, now on the town, and Art, a vagrant,' and ultimately 'kept on drinking.' 'Mr. Flood's Party' describes an elderly man who climbs a hill to drink alone, compared to 'Roland's ghost winding a silent horn,' looking down on a town whose doors have closed to him.

'Luke Havergal' commands its title figure to 'Go to the western gate' where 'the vines cling crimson on the wall' and declares 'hell is more than half of paradise.' 'How Annandale Went Out' is a sonnet about euthanasia where a physician recalls using 'a slight kind of engine.' 'The House on the Hill' is a villanelle alternating 'They are all gone away' and 'There is nothing more to say.' 'Eros Turannos' features a woman who stays with her husband because 'all her doubts of what he says are dimmed with what she knows of days.'

Robinson won three Pulitzer Prizes and his collections include The Children of the Night and The Man Against the Sky. Louise Bogan wrote a critical study titled Tilbury Town and Beyond.

cards.front: Poem: a 'gentleman from sole to crown,' admired and envied, 'one calm summer night, went home and put a bullet through his head'
Poem: a 'child of scorn' who 'loved the Medici / Albeit he had never seen one,' dreamed of Thebes and Camelot, and 'kept on drinking'
Poem: an old man compared to 'Roland's ghost winding a silent horn' stops in the middle of the road to have a drink with himself and sings 'for auld lang syne'
Poem: commands the title character to 'Go to the western gate' where 'the vines cling crimson on the wall'; declares 'hell is more than half of paradise'
Poem: a sonnet about euthanasia; a physician used 'a slight kind of engine' and asks 'You wouldn't hang me? I thought not'
cards.back: How Annandale Went Out (Edwin Arlington Robinson)

cards.front: Author: set poems in the fictional Tilbury Town, modeled after Gardiner, Maine; residents include Cliff Klingenhagen who drinks wormwood
